mnemonics.title: C-A-L-M A-D-D-I-T (Vector Space Axioms)
mnemonics.content: C(losure) A(ssociativity) L(inear combination/Commutativity) M(ultiplicative identity) - A(dditive identity) D(inverse) D(istributivity) I(dentity) T(wo operations). These are the 10 axioms every vector space must satisfy!

mnemonics.title: NAC - The Subspace Test
mnemonics.content: N-A-C: Non-empty (contains zero vector), Additive closure (u+v in subspace), Closed under scalar multiplication (c*u in subspace). Pass all three = Valid Subspace!

mnemonics.title: L-I-D (Linear Independence Definition)
mnemonics.content: Linear Independence: Only trivial solution (all scalars = 0) exists when c1v1 + c2v2 + ... + cnvn = 0. If any non-zero scalar works = Dependent!

mnemonics.title: Spanning Set Reminder
mnemonics.content: Span = All possible linear combinations. Think: Can I make EVERY vector in the space using these building blocks? If yes, it spans!

acronyms.usage: When checking if a set with operations is a vector space, verify all 10 VSAs (closure, commutativity, associativity, identity, inverses, distributivity)

acronyms.expansion: Non-empty, Additive closure, Closed under scalar multiplication
acronyms.usage: The three conditions to verify if a subset is a subspace of a vector space
Expression: c1v1 + c2v2 + ... + cnvn where ci are scalars. Used in span, independence, basis definitions
Check if vectors are LID: Can one be written as combination of others? If yes = dependent. If only trivial solution = independent

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an infinite 3D room where every point is a vector. The room has rules (addition = moving from one point to another, scalar multiplication = stretching/shrinking). R² = flat sheet, R³ = your room, Rⁿ = n-dimensional hyper-room!

visualTips.concept: Subspace as a Plane Through Origin
visualTips.visualization: Think of R³ (3D space). A subspace through origin is like: the whole space (3D), a flat plane passing through origin (2D), or a straight line through origin (1D). Cannot be a plane that doesn't pass through origin - it would miss the zero vector!
Imagine a flashlight (span) starting from origin. One vector = line of light. Two non-parallel vectors = plane of light. The span fills everything those vectors can 'reach' from the origin.
Think of basis as the fewest number of colored paints needed to create every color. Like RGB can make any color, i1, i2, i3 can make any 3D vector. Too few = can't span, too many = become dependent!

analogies.analogy: Think of a playground with specific rules: you can add any two playground elements together (combine swings), multiply by a number (use swing 3 times faster). As long as you stay in playground and follow rules - it's a valid vector space!

A specific area within the playground. Just like a basketball court is part of the school ground, a subspace must: contain the center point (zero vector), if you're in the court and walk somewhere, you stay on court (additive closure), if you practice a move 5 times you still on court (scalar closure)
Team members with unique skills. If one person can do what another does, you have redundancy (dependent). Independent vectors = each brings something NEW to the table, like specialists in a team!

Recipe cookbook! The span of ingredients = all possible dishes you can cook with them. Salt + Pepper can make many dishes but not everything. Salt + Pepper + Flour + Water = much bigger span (can make bread, pasta, etc.)
Basis = minimum number of RGB pixels needed to create any color on screen. Dimension = how many color pixels you need. R² = 2 coordinates (x,y), R³ = 3 coordinates (x,y,z). Like degrees of freedom!
